---
title: "Bases and dimensions"
description: "Bases, dimension, and the dimension formula for subspace sums — an augmented summary of Axler's LADR §2.B–2.C."
date: "2026-06-03"
date-modified: "2026-06-18"
categories: ["mathematics", "linear-algebra", "ladr"]
execute:
    echo: true
---

{{< include ../../_macros.qmd >}}

This is an augmented summary of Sections 2.B and 2.C of [Linear Algebra Done Right](https://linear.axler.net/index.html) by Sheldon Axler.

As in the previous section, we assume that $\mb{F}\in\{\mathbb{R},\mathbb{C}\}$.

## Basis
We have come across linearly independent sets and spanning sets. What if a set is linearly independent *and* spans $V$? Then it is called a **basis** of $V$.

<details class="example">
<summary>Standard basis of $\mb{F}^n$</summary>

The set of vectors

$$(1,0,\cdots,0),(0,1,0,\cdots,0),\cdots,(0,\cdots,0,1)\in\mb{F}^n$$

is linearly independent and spans $\mb{F}^n$. It is called the **standard basis** of $\mb{F}^n$.

</details>

<details class="example">
<summary>Basis of $\mathcal{P}_n(\mb{F})$</summary>

The set of vectors $1,z,\cdots,z^n$ is linearly independent and spans $\mathcal{P}_n(\mb{F})$, and is thereby a basis of $\mathcal{P}_n(\mb{F})$.

</details>

<details class="example">
<summary>Non-standard basis of $\mb{F}^2$</summary>

Consider the set $(1,2),(3,5)\in\mb{F}^2$. It is linearly independent because one vector is not a scalar multiple of the other. 

So by the Steinitz exchange lemma, it can be exchanged with the standard basis $(1,0),(0,1)$ while preserving the span of the latter, namely $\mb{F}^2$.

As a linearly independent set that spans $\mb{F}^n$, the set is a basis of $\mb{F}^2$.

</details>

<details class="example">
<summary>Basis for subspace of $\mb{F}^3$ with linear constraint</summary>

Consider the subspace of $\mb{F}^3$ defined as

$$\{(x,y,z):x+y+z=0\}.$$

Any vector in this subspace can be expressed as 

$$(x,y,-x-y)=(x+y)(1,0,-1)+(-y)(1,-1,0),$$

and the set $(1,0,-1),(1,-1,0)$ is linearly independent because one vector is not a scalar multiple of the other. Therefore, this set is a basis of the subspace.

</details>

By convention, we say that the basis of $\{0\}$ is the empty set, with $\sum_{v\in\emptyset}v=0$.

#### Unique decomposition under basis
:::{#thm-basis-unique}
The set of vectors $v_1,\cdots,v_n$ forms a basis of $V$ if and only if every $v\in V$ can be uniquely represented as

$$v=a_1v_1+\cdots+a_nv_n.$$

:::

:::{.informal}
Every vector has one, and only one, set of coordinates in a basis.
:::

:::{.proof-idea}
Recall that linear independence implies unique decomposition of the vectors in the span.
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

Suppose that the set $v_1,\cdots,v_n$ is a basis of $V$. Then the set spans $V$ and so *every* vector of $V$ can be written as *some* linear combination. As the set is linearly independent, these combinations are unique.

Suppose every vector of $V$ can be uniquely decomposed as a linear combination of $v_1,\cdots,v_n$. Tautologically, the set spans $V$. As $0\in V$ is uniquely decomposed, the set is also linearly independent. So $v_1,\cdots,v_n$ is a basis.

</details>

From the subspace sum point of view, if $v_1,\cdots,v_n$ is a basis of $V$, then

$$\{av_1\}\oplus\cdots\oplus\{av_n\}=V.$$

#### Making basis
###### From spanning set
:::{#thm-basis-from-spanning}
We can obtain a basis from *any* spanning set by pruning the set.
:::

:::{.proof-idea}
Sequentially remove redundant vectors.
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

Suppose that $U$ is a spanning set. If it is not linearly independent, then there is a redundant vector in the set whose removal does not affect the span. Remove it. 

We can repeat until the modified set is linearly independent. Since a set with a single non-zero vector is always linearly independent, so the process terminates before reaching the linearly dependent $\{0\}$. 

When we achieve linear independence, the set still spans $V$, and is therefore a basis.

</details>

:::{#thm-basis-exists}
As a corollary, we can show that every finite-dimensional vector space always has a basis.
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

If we know that a vector space is finite-dimensional, that means that it has a finite spanning set, from which we can create a basis by pruning.

</details>

###### From linearly independent set
Does this result hold from the other direction? It does, as long as the space is finite-dimensional.

:::{#thm-basis-from-independent}
*Any* linearly independent set in a finite-dimensional space can be extended to form a basis.
:::

:::{.proof-idea}
Utilise the same method as the proof that a subspace of a finite-dimensional vector space is also finite. 
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

Suppose that we have a linearly independent set of vectors $v_1,\cdots,v_n$. If its span is not equal to $V$, we may append to the set a vector not in the span. Then the extended set is still linearly independent. 

This process must terminate before the set becomes larger than a spanning set of $V$. So after a number of steps, the span must be equal to $V$, at which point the extended set both is linearly independent and spans $V$.

</details>

So instead of pruning a spanning set, we can also find a basis by growing the empty set.

#### Decomposing a vector space into a direct sum
:::{#thm-complement-exists}
Suppose that $U$ is a subspace of a finite-dimensional $V$. Then there always exists another subspace $W$ such that $V=U\oplus W$.
:::

:::{.informal}
Every subspace has a "direct sum buddy" (although this buddy is not unique), which is a "maximally different" subspace (i.e. only intersects at $0$) such that the pair adds up to the full space. 
:::

:::{.proof-idea}
At first, we might panic and think that we should subtract $U$ from $V$ somehow. But recall that we already know of a direct sum decomposition of $V$, namely in terms of its basis.
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

Let $u_1,\cdots,u_m$ be a basis of $U$. This basis exists because every subspace of a finite-dimensional space is also finite-dimensional, and every finite-dimensional space has a basis.

Let us extend this set with $w_1,\cdots,w_n$ to form a basis of $V$. Then we have

$$
V=\{au_1\}\oplus\cdots\oplus\{au_m\}\oplus\{aw_1\}\oplus\cdots\oplus\{aw_n\}=U\oplus W,
$$

where $W=\{aw_1\}\oplus\cdots\oplus\{aw_n\}$. 

</details>

This subspace $W$ is called a **complement subspace** of $U$. As it depends on the basis chosen, it is not unique. (This connects to the fact that the direct sum does not cancel.) For example, if $V=\mathbb{R}^2$ and $U$ is some line through the origin, then the complement subspace $W$ can be any line through the origin not equal to $U$. 

#### Basis for direct sum
:::{#thm-direct-sum-basis}
Suppose that $V=U\oplus W$, where $u_1,\cdots,u_m$ and $w_1,\cdots,w_n$ are bases of $U$ and $W$ respectively. Then the union of the two bases is a basis of $V$.
:::

:::{.informal}
We can form a basis of $V$ by a "divide and conquer" approach, by finding the bases for the direct summands and taking their union.
:::

:::{.proof-idea}
If the decomposition into the proposed basis is not unique, then neither is the decomposition into the direct summands. 
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

Let $v\in V$. We need to show that a decomposition into the union exists, and that it is unique.

Existence: Since $V=U+W$, we always have $v=u+w$ for some $u\in U$ and $w\in W$. As a basis spans its subspace, there exist $a_1,\cdots, a_m$ and $b_1,\cdots, b_n$ such that $u=a_1u_1+\cdots+a_mu_m$ and $w=b_1w_1+\cdots+b_nw_n$. Therefore, we have

$$v=a_1u_1+\cdots+a_mu_m+b_1w_1+\cdots+b_nw_n.$$

Uniqueness: Suppose that $v\in V$ can be decomposed in two ways as

$$
\begin{aligned}
v&=a_1u_1+\cdots+a_mu_m+b_1w_1+\cdots+b_nw_n\\
&=a'_1u_1+\cdots+a'_mu_m+b'_1w_1+\cdots+b'_nw_n.
\end{aligned}
$$

We note that 

$$
\begin{gather}
a_1u_1+\cdots+a_mu_m,\ a'_1u_1+\cdots+a'_mu_m\in U\\
b_1w_1+\cdots+b_nw_n,\ b'_1w_1+\cdots+b'_nw_n\in W,
\end{gather}
$$

so if it is the case that

$$
\begin{gather}
a_1u_1+\cdots+a_mu_m\neq a'_1u_1+\cdots+a'_mu_m\\
\text{or}\quad b_1w_1+\cdots+b_nw_n\neq b'_1w_1+\cdots+b'_nw_n,
\end{gather}
$$ 

then there are two distinct ways in which $v$ can be decomposed in the form $u+w$. This contradicts the directness of $U+W$. So the first pair must sum to the same $u$, and the second pair to the same $w$. By the linear independence of the $u_i$s and the $w_i$s, this implies $a_i=a'_i$ and $b_i=b'_i$. 

</details>

## Dimension
By the Steinitz exchange lemma, we saw that a spanning set is never smaller than a linearly independent set. So if a basis does exist, then all the other bases of $V$ must have the same cardinality. Otherwise, a basis *qua* linearly independent set will be larger than another basis *qua* spanning set.

This unique cardinality of the bases is called the **dimension** of the vector space, and is denoted $\dim V$.

Because of our convention where the basis of $\{0\}$ is the empty set, its dimension is $0$. 

<details class="example">
<summary>Dimension for $\mb{F}^n$ and $\mathcal{P}_n(\mb{F})$</summary>

We have seen that the $n$ standard basis vectors form a basis of $\mb{F}^n$, and so $\dim\mb{F}^n=n$. 

Meanwhile, $1,z,\cdots,z^n$ forms a basis of $\mathcal{P}_n(\mb{F})$, and so $\dim\mathcal{P}_n(\mb{F})=n+1$.

</details>

<details class="example">
<summary>Dimensions of $\mathbb{R}^2$ and $\mathbb{C}$</summary>

The dimension of $\mathbb{R}^2$ over $\mathbb{R}$ is $2$, with one basis being $(1,0),(0,1)$. However, the dimension of $\mathbb{C}$ over $\mathbb{C}$ is $1$, because any complex number can be reached from a non-zero $z\in\mathbb{C}$ by multiplication with a complex number.

This difference in dimensions arises even though $\mathbb{C}$ over $\mathbb{R}$ (not over $\mathbb{C}$!) is isomorphic to $\mathbb{R}^2$ over $\mathbb{R}$.

</details>

:::{#thm-dim-bounds}
The size of a linearly independent set in $V$ is a lower bound for $\dim V$. Likewise, the size of a spanning set is an upper bound for $\dim V$.
:::

#### Adding vector to set of linearly independent vectors
:::{#thm-}
Let $v_1,\cdots,v_n$ be a set of linearly independent vectors in $V$, and consider an arbitrary $w\in V$. Then we have

$$\dim\text{span}(v_1+w,\cdots,v_n+w)\geq n-1.$$

:::

:::{.proof-idea}
Consider the first difference of linearly independent vectors.
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

It suffices to show that the span has a set of linearly independent vectors of size $n-1$.

Previously, we have shown that if $v_1,\cdots,v_n$ is linearly independent, so is their first difference

$$v_1-v_2,\cdots,v_{n-1}-v_n,v_n.$$

This means that $v_1-v_2,\cdots,v_{n-1}-v_n$ is linearly independent as well, and each of these vectors are in $\text{span}(v_1+w,\cdots,v_n+w)$. (It is the first difference of the vectors in the set.) 

As $\text{span}(v_1+w,\cdots,v_n+w)$ contains a linearly independent set of size $n-1$, its dimension must be at least $n-1$.

</details>

#### Dimension as limits to cardinality
###### For linearly independent sets
:::{#thm-independent-dim-basis}
Any linearly independent set of size $\dim V$ is a basis of $V$.
:::

:::{.proof-idea}
This set should not be grow-able.
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

If it does not span $V$, then we can add to the set a vector not in the span, and the set will remain linearly independent. So there will be a linearly independent set larger than a spanning set (namely, a basis), which is a contradiction. So all such linearly independent sets span $V$ and so are bases.

</details>

<details class="example">
<summary>Basis of polynomials equal at two points</summary>

We wish to show that a basis of

$$U=\{p\in\mathcal{P}_4(\mb{F}):p(x_1)=p(x_2)\}$$

for some unequal $x_1,x_2\in\mb{F}$ is given by

$$
1,(x-x_1)(x-x_2),(x-x_1)^2(x-x_2),(x-x_1)^3(x-x_2).
$$

It is easy to show linear independence. In the equation

$$a_1+a_2(x-x_1)(x-x_2)+a_3(x-x_1)^2(x-x_2)+a_4(x-x_1)^3(x-x_2)=0,$$

the scalar $a_4$ has to vanish to eliminate $x^4$, so $a_3$ in turn has to vanish to eliminate $x^3$, and so on.

Now, it is unnecessary to show that the set spans $U$ to show that it is a basis of $U$. Because $U\neq \mathcal{P}_4(\mb{F})$, we have $\dim U<5$ (see @thm-full-dim-subspace). Since we have found a linearly independent set of 4 vectors, the only possibility is that $\dim U=4$, with this set being a basis of $U$.

Furthermore, it can be extended to a basis of $V$ by adding the "missing degree": $x$.

</details>

<details class="example">
<summary>Basis of $\mathcal{P}_n(\mb{F})$</summary>

The above argument for showing linear independence can be generalised. Consider any set

$$p_0,p_1,\cdots,p_n\in\mathcal{P}(\mb{F})$$

where $p_i$ has degree $i$. The analogous argument shows the set to be linearly independent. Since $\dim\mathcal{P}_n(\mb{F})=n+1$, this set is a basis of $\mathcal{P}_n(\mb{F})$[^1].

</details>

###### For spanning sets
:::{#thm-spanning-dim-basis}
Any spanning set of cardinality $\dim V$ is a basis of $V$.
:::

:::{.proof-idea}
This set should not be prune-able.
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

If it is linearly dependent, we can remove a vector from the set so that the set still spans $V$. Then there is a spanning set smaller than a linearly independent set (namely, a basis), which is a contradiction. So all such spanning sets are linearly independent and thereby are bases.

</details>

###### Subspace with same dimension
:::{#thm-full-dim-subspace}
If $U$ is a subspace of $V$ with $\dim U=\dim V$, then we have $U=V$.
:::

:::{.informal}
If we find that a subspace has the same number of basis vectors as $V$, we know that this subspace is just $V$.
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

If $\dim U=\dim V$, then a basis $u_1,\cdots,u_n$ of $U$ is also a basis of $V$. Therefore,

$$\text{span}(u_1,\cdots,u_n)=U=V.$$

</details>

<details class="example">
<summary>Enumeration of subspaces of $\mathbb{R}^3$</summary>

We are now at a position to show that the only subspaces of $\mathbb{R}^3$ are what they are. The basis for a subspace $U$ has 3 linearly independent vectors at most, because $\dim\mathbb{R}^3=3$. So the possibilities are

- $\dim U=0$: $U=\{0\}$.
- $\dim U=1$: $U=\{tv:t\in\mathbb{R}\}$ for a non-zero $v$, which is a line through the origin.
- $\dim U=2$: $U=\{tv_1+sv_2:t,s\in\mathbb{R}\}$, where $v_1$ and $v_2$ are linearly independent, which is a plane through the origin.
- $\dim U=3$, in which case $U$ must be $\mathbb{R}^3$.

</details>

#### Dimension of a subspace sum
###### Sum of two subspaces
Let's consider the sum $U_1+U_2$ (not necessarily direct), where $U_1$ and $U_2$ are subspaces of a finite-dimensional $V$. Is its dimension expressible in terms of those of the constituent subspaces? Yes, and with a rather familiar expression.

:::{#thm-dim-sum-formula}
The dimension of $U_1+U_2$ is given by

$$\dim (U_1+U_2)=\dim U_1+\dim U_2-\dim (U_1\cap U_2).$$

:::

:::{.informal}
The dimension of $U_1+U_2$ has the same form as the inclusion-exclusion principle (which describes the size of the union of two sets).
:::

:::{.proof-idea}
Extend a basis of $U_1\cap U_2$ to bases of $U_1$ and $U_2$.
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

An intersection of subspaces is also a subspace, so let $y_1,\cdots,y_n$ be a basis of $U_1\cap U_2$. This set is a linearly independent subset of $U_1$, and can be grown into a basis of $U_1$. Let us call the corresponding additions as $x_1,\cdots,x_m$. Likewise, the vectors added while growing the set into a basis of $U_2$ will be called $z_1,\cdots,z_p$.

We will show that

$$x_1,\cdots,x_m,y_1,\cdots,y_n,z_1,\cdots,z_p$$

is a basis of $U_1+U_2$. Every vector in $U_1$ can be written as a linear combination of $x_1,\cdots,x_m,y_1,\cdots,y_n$. Likewise, every vector in $U_2$ is a linear combination of $y_1\cdots,y_n,z_1,\cdots,z_p$. So the set does span $U_1+U_2$.

Now suppose that

$$a_1x_1+\cdots+a_mx_m+b_1y_1+\cdots+b_ny_n+c_1z_1+\cdots+c_pz_p=0.$$

We need to show that the coefficients are necessarily all zero. Moving the remaining terms to the other side of equality, we see that $c_1z_1+\cdots+c_pz_p\in U_1$. This means that it is an element of $U_1\cap U_2$, so there exist $d_i$ such that

$$c_1z_1+\cdots+c_pz_p=d_1y_1+\cdots+d_ny_n.$$

Plugging it in above, we have

$$a_1x_1+\cdots+a_mx_m+(b_1+d_1)y_1+\cdots+(b_n+d_n)y_n=0.$$

By the linear independence of $x_1,\cdots,x_m,y_1,\cdots,y_n$, this implies that the $a_i$s are zero. In an analogous manner, we can find that the $c_i$s are zero. Finally, we are left with 

$$b_1y_1+\cdots+b_ny_n=0,$$

and the $b_i$s are also zero by the linear independence of the $y_i$s.

Therefore, the above-mentioned set is a basis of $U_1+U_2$, and we have

$$\dim (U_1+U_2)=\dim U_1+\dim U_2-\dim (U_1\cap U_2).$$

</details>

<details class="example">
<summary>When sum of dimensions is dimension of sum</summary>

Suppose that $U+W=V$, with $\dim U=3$, $\dim W=5$ and $\dim V=8$. Then we immediately know that $V=U\oplus W$, because the above formula shows that $\dim(U\cap W)=0$, which in turn implies $U\cap W=\{0\}$.

</details>

<details class="example">
<summary>When dimensions preclude direct sum</summary>

Suppose that $U$ and $W$ are 4-dimensional subspaces of $\mathbb{C}^6$. At most, the dimension of $U+W$ is $6$, in which case the dimension of their intersection is

$$\dim(U\cap W)=\dim U+\dim W-\dim\mathbb{C}^6=2.$$

If the dimension of $U+W$ is smaller, then that of the intersection becomes larger. So the sum cannot be direct. (Also, the intersection always contains two vectors that are not scalar multiples of one another.)

</details>

###### Sum of three subspaces
Does the analogy to the inclusion-exclusion principle hold for 3 subspaces? That is, is it the case that

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dim(U_1+U_2+&U_3)=\dim U_1+\dim U_2+\dim U_3\\
&-\dim (U_1\cap U_2)-\dim (U_2\cap U_3)-\dim (U_1\cap U_3)+\dim(U_1\cap U_2\cap U_3)?
\end{aligned}
$$

:::{#thm-dim-three-fails}
The analogue of the inclusion-exclusion principle generally does not hold for the sum of three subspaces.
:::

:::{.proof-idea}
The analogous 3-set inclusion-exclusion principle expression is obtained by applying the 2-set expression repeatedly, so let's try doing that for the dimension sum:

$$
\dim(U_1+U_2+U_3)=\dim U_1+\dim(U_2+U_3)-\dim(U_1\cap(U_2+U_3)).
$$

For an analogous derivation to go through, we need the following distributivity to hold:

$$U_1\cap(U_2+U_3)=U_1\cap U_2+U_1\cap U_3.$$

Is this true? Intuitively, we can imagine a scenario where $U_2+U_3$ contains $U_1$ but both $U_1\cap U_2$ and $U_1\cap U_3$ are trivial subspaces, in which case the sum afterwards is "too late". 
:::

An example of this is provided by any three distinct lines through the origin on $\mathbb{R}^2$. We can check that this example breaks the proposed formula for $\dim(U_1+U_2+U_3)$, because the LHS is 2 and the RHS is 3. 

###### General inequality
:::{#thm-dim-sum-inequality}
While there is no general expression for the dimension of a sum of $n$ subspaces, at least we have the inequality

$$\dim(U_1+\cdots+U_n)\leq\dim U_1+\cdots+\dim U_n.$$
:::

:::{.proof-idea}
This can be inductively shown with the two-subspace-sum formula.
:::

###### Direct sum
:::{#thm-dim-direct-sum}
For finite subspaces $U_1,\cdots,U_n$ that form a direct sum, we have

$$\dim(U_1\oplus\cdots\oplus U_n)=\dim U_1+\cdots+\dim U_n.$$

:::

:::{.informal}
We can find the dimension of $V$ by a divide-and-conquer approach if we have a direct sum decomposition (which is really just a corollary of @thm-direct-sum-basis).
:::

:::{.proof-idea}
Previously, we showed that the union of the bases of $U$ and $W$ is a basis of $U\oplus W$. So when we have a direct sum, then we have the equality

$$\dim U\oplus W=\dim U+\dim W.$$

The result for an arbitrary number of subspaces follows by induction.
:::



[^1]: This result can be used to show that differentiation as a linear map on polynomials is surjective. Let $D$ be any linear map from $\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{R})$ to $\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{R})$ with the property $\deg Dp=(\deg p)-1$ (of which the differentiation map is one). Then $Dx,Dx^2,\cdots,Dx^{n+1}$ spans $\mathcal{P}_n(\mathbb{R})$, so for any $p\in\mathcal{P}_n(\mathbb{R})$, there exist coefficients such that

    $$p=a_1Dx+a_2Dx^2+\cdots+a_{n+1}Dx^{n+1}=D(a_1x+a_2x^2+\cdots+a_{n+1}x^{n+1}).$$

    So a polynomial of any degree has an "integral" polynomial.
